title: Jiang Xueqin (clip): Why Does the West Have Such Terrible Leaders?
full_text: Why are there so such terrible leaders at the moment? Uh not just in the United States but across Europe as well. I mean terrible in terms of there's no there's no there's no vision. There's no strategy. It's it's it's very dishonest. It's all about managing narratives. It Yeah. Well, overall we don't see any leaders of the past. And again, this is Europe where you began with the piece of Espalia. you came came up with you know modern diplomacy. Uh this is uh yeah I don't see any leaders again in Europe today which uh seems to be able to get the continent back on track which well then you kind of hand over the mantle to any well crazy leader who any strongman coming along claiming that he can fix the system if he just pushes the old order aside. I think one of the greatest lies in our lives is that the meritocracy um promotes the best leaders. Okay. So if you went to Yale, if you went to Harvard, you went to Ox Oxbridge, if you went to the to the suborn um um then you are the best and the brightest and therefore you should rule. You know, I went to Yale, okay? And I can tell you right now that you go to place like Yale, you're not taught how to think critically. You're not taught how to be imaginative, how to have empathy, how to be resilient. You're taught how to speak in a very nice articulate manner and to conform to the status quo. Okay? So if you look at the ruling class in America, the vast majority of people in Washington DC of the financial elite in in Wall Street, Silicon Valley, um um the media, okay, the universities, they all went to a few schools and as a result they have a ve they live in a bubble. they they see the world in a very um jaded manner. Um and and and so they're unable to empathize with the suffering of ordinary people. They they become disconnected with the ordinary people and they have actually they cannot possibly imagine um what life is like for just a blueco collar worker. That's that's one. Number two is the problem with meritocracy is that if you think that you deserve what you have because you worked hard, then you also believe that other people who are poor deserve what they got because they're lazy. Okay? So, the government should not help these people. The government should help immigrants. The government should help minor minorities. The government should help women because these are historically persecuted groups. But they should we should not help ordinary um white men because they deserve what they got. because you're stupid. I worked hard in school and so I got I got a deal. You didn't work hard in school and so you should be a guardsman making very little money. You should be unemployed. Okay. So there's just this contempt. There's just this um visual disontempt for the ordinary people among the elite that drives a lot of their policies. Okay. And and then um the third thing and this is the most important is that when you go to a place like Yale, you've succeeded all your life. And so you're not capable of humility. You're not capable of self-reflection. Okay? So in 2016 when Trump won, this was a pivotal moment in Western history. At this point, the elite could say, "Listen, why did Trump win?" Well, it was probably because of inequality. It was probably because of those um you know, because we bailed out Wall Street in 2008, right? we should build out the home homeowners, but instead we built out Wall Street. So, it's our fault that uh the people elected Trump. This is a wake up call for us. It's time for the elite to start working for the masses again. Instead, what happened? Instead, the elite said, "Oh, it's because Trump's a Putin spy. It's because he's a spy." And Russia gate, you know, this this hoax will prove that he's a spy. Okay? So, it's not our fault. Oh, also Trump cheated. Uh, you know, it's it's it's it's because of Facebook. So they found also pretext to disguise the fact that Trump won because of mismanagement by the elite. Okay? And and so we have this elite class in America and Europe who are extremely arrogant. They live in their own bubble. They're mediocre, but they think that they're superior. Um and they're incapable of any humility, any empathy. And that's why we are where we are. Well, when you said this, I just u clicked open a quote by Professor Richard Roerty because he was making a similar point, but back in the '9s cuz when economic liberalism and globalism of globalization of the 1990s were gaining speed, he he wrote this um paragraph in his book which I thought was great. And uh essentially this was uh yeah predicting what excessive economic liberalism under globalization that it would produce some uh political radicalism. So he and he he wrote this a quote um members of labor unions and unorganized and unskilled workers will sooner or later realize that their government is not even trying to prevent wages from sinking or to prevent jobs from being exported. Around the same time, they will realize that suburban white collar workers, themselves, desperately afraid of being downsized, are not going to let themselves be taxed to provide social benefits for anyone else. At that point, something will crack. The suburban electorate will decide that the system has failed and start looking around for strong men to vote for. Someone willing to assure them that once he's elected, the smug bureaucrats, tricky lawyers, overpaid bond salesmen, and post-modernist professors will no longer be calling the shots. Once the strongman takes office, no one can predict what will happen." This is uh >> This is 1998. This is >> Yeah. You better describe what happened. >> Yep. This is exactly what happened. Exactly. >> So, so this rooted in globalization and the excessive of economic liberalism. Is this what caused the problems which introduced the strong men or how? >> Yeah. You look I mean look, you go back to 1970s and you were living in the west at that at that time, right? Australia, Canada, Europe, America. You had the best life, right? because you just worked in a factory and your wife could just set a home and be and be a a um um you know a uh just just be uh just look after the kids. You had three kids. Uh you had two cars. You could vacation in the winter and in the summer. You had a cottage probably. Uh your all three of your kids could go to university, right? And we're talking about like like just a factory worker. Uh and like you this is a single household, okay? But then you had the Reagan revolution. You had the rise of Margaret Thatcher in uh in in Britain. And then you had the rise of neoliberalism which allowed for the congregation the consolidation of wealth in uh in the 1%. You also had globalization. So wealth was being transferred from the developing world into the developed world to create the speculative financial products like derivatives, right? Um so it went all wrong with the Reagan Reagan revolution. um and the rise of neoliberalism and that's the root of all the issues that we have today. >> So what are we looking at there then? Because uh I think people are maybe often um yeah they get too focused on the whatever Trump says today or this week. But uh what do you see in the wider again take a step back are we looking at the meltdown of the existing international order the both the economic the security aspect I mean the political system what what are you seeing here >> yeah so the idea that Mark Carney has right like you know we'll just pivot to China it's not going to work um and the reason why is that China doesn't want to be the hegeimon of the world it doesn't want to be the military super power it doesn't want to control the seas the way America does. Okay, the American Empire is is a unique time in history. It was really not sustainable. Uh the idea that one country is able to control the entire planet um through te for through for technology through the military and for finance that was just not sustainable in the long term and it really hurt America. it uh it caused America to be over overextended to be overleveraged um and that caused a lot of economic issues in uh in America which allowed for the rise of Trump. So we are just witnessing the breakdown of the multilateral world and there's no replacement. Okay. And what this means is more conflicts around the world uh specifically between Russia and America. But uh you know there's talk of America attacking Iran at any point. Okay. That's one thing we'll see. Second thing is we will see the breakdown of global trade. So um you know more countries will retreat onto themselves and impose tariffs to pro protect their local uh economies. You will also see environmental catastrophes. You know so there's this giant snowstorm that engulf America and this is going to be the new normal. Um you're going to see radical climate change over the next next few years that are going to that are going to strain the country's resources which forces them to be uh more militaristic. uh for example in the case of Japan uh but also much more protectionist. Okay. So so so this is the reality that that we're moving towards and it's very similar to um um basically the world in 1930s you know the great depression right before the start of World War II. So it seems like all sides are moving towards a major global conflict and right now it's really a question of like which who's who's going to take which sides. You have Russia on one side you have America on another side. It's really a question of like you know what where will the other powers join and unfortunately I think what's going to happen is that it's going to be complete chaos where the um sides won't be so clear and it's really um different countries will choose different alliances but these will be shifting alliances over time so China will try to play both sides um you UK Europe will try to play both sides so so it'll be a very dynamic geopolitical struggle All [snorts] >> well it reminds me about what uh Kennan wrote in this policy planning staff uh memorandum in 1948. He was making the point that the US has about 50% of the world's wealth but only 6% of its population. So this would uh well more or less it would require some manipulation of the system because people would have be envious and resentful.
